2025 · ICLR 2025 · Benchmarks & Evals

WildBench: Benchmarking LLMs with Challenging Tasks from Real Users in the Wild

An LLM-as-judge benchmark distilled straight from a million real WildChat conversations: 1,024 hard, multi-turn tasks graded with per-instance checklists and a three-baseline pairwise metric with a length-penalty tiebreaker, reaching a 0.98 Pearson correlation with Chatbot Arena’s human-voted Elo on top models — ahead of Arena-Hard’s 0.91 and AlpacaEval 2.0’s 0.87/0.89.

♪ listen — audio episode
3-voice deep dive · mp3 ↗ · rss feed ↗
Bill Yuchen Lin, Yuntian Deng, Khyathi Chandu, Faeze Brahman, Abhilasha Ravichander, Valentina Pyatkin, Nouha Dziri, Ronan Le Bras, Yejin Choi · Allen Institute for AI (Choi also University of Washington)
venue ICLR 2025 citations 227 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By mid-2024 the LLM-as-judge landscape had a diversity problem. MT-Bench has only 80 hand-crafted questions. AlpacaEval draws 805 tasks from Alpaca-demo-style instructions, but skews heavily toward simple information-seeking requests — the paper counts over 20 tasks that redundantly ask for a recipe, and just 6% coding / 3% math coverage. Arena-Hard corrects the difficulty problem by sampling 500 real Chatbot Arena prompts, but overcorrects on breadth: coding & debugging alone accounts for over 57% of its content. None of the three reflect the natural mix of what people actually ask chatbots to do.

Chatbot Arena itself is the obvious ground truth — real users, live pairwise votes, Elo ratings — but it "has notable limitations, such as high labor costs, the inability to deliver real-time results, a lack of data transparency, and the challenge of fairly evaluating all models with the same data." It cannot be run on every checkpoint a lab produces.

WildBench's fix is to mine the gap directly: take AI2's WildChat dataset — over one million real human-chatbot conversations — and distill a benchmark whose task distribution matches actual usage rather than an author's imagination or Arena's own coding-heavy skew.

// how it works

The method

Mining hard tasks from WildChat

Starting from WildChat's million-plus logs, the pipeline filters aggressively: drop queries under 10 or over 3,000 tokens; drop conversations with more than five user turns; keep English only; remove toxic conversations; deduplicate near-identical queries via SentenceBERT cosine similarity above 0.9; and keep only the last conversation per unique device to avoid over-sampling any one user's habits. GPT-4-Turbo, Claude-3-Sonnet, and Claude-3-Opus then each rate every surviving task's difficulty on a five-point scale, and anything every model calls "easy" or "very easy" is dropped. From what's left, 1,500 tasks are sampled to match the original WildChat topic distribution, GPT-4-Turbo summarizes each query's intent to help human reviewers filter out nonsensical tasks, and the process bottoms out at 1,024 final tasks for WildBench V2 (up from a V1 released in March 2024). To limit contamination, the team coordinated with the WildChat maintainers so the sampled tasks are withheld from WildChat's own public release.

The curated set is also structurally different from its predecessors — it allows up to 5 conversational turns (over 20% of WildBench examples have 2+ turns) and averages 978.5 characters per final query and 3,402.1 characters of full prompt+history, versus AlpacaEval and Arena-Hard's single-turn ~165–406 character prompts.

Two metrics, both checklist-guided

Rather than a single free-form judge prompt, every task gets an instance-specific checklist of 5–10 interpretable questions, drafted by combining GPT-4-Turbo's and Claude-3-Opus's outputs (to dilute single-model bias) and manually reviewed. The judge is prompted chain-of-thought style — analyze, then decide — using the checklist as a rubric before reaching a verdict.

WB-Reward is pairwise: instead of one fixed baseline (the norm in prior work), the test model is compared against three baselines at different strength tiers — GPT-4-Turbo-0429, Claude-3-Haiku, and Llama-2-70B-chat — producing a natural three-tier grouping of the field. Each comparison returns one of five outcomes:

reward(X vs Y) ∈ { +1, +0.5, 0, −0.5, −1 } // much better · slightly better · tie · slightly worse · much worse — WB-Reward(Mix) averages across all 3 baselines

To blunt length bias, any "slightly better/worse" outcome is downgraded to a tie whenever the winning response is longer than the losing one by more than K characters (K is user-configurable on the live leaderboard; K=∞ disables the penalty; the paper's own ablation found K=500 gives the best correlation with human judgments among {100, 200, 500, 1000, ∞}). Note the penalty only touches the two "slightly" outcomes — "much better/worse" verdicts pass through untouched regardless of length gap.

WB-Score grades each response individually from 1–10 against the same checklist, then rescales around a "5 = borderline acceptable" midpoint:

S′ = (S − 5) × 2 // raw 1–10 score S → rescaled S′, spreading the distribution around 0

WB-Score is the cheaper metric — about $5 and 10 minutes per model, versus 3–4× that for WB-Reward's three-baseline comparisons — so both ship on the public leaderboard and users pick based on budget.

// what they found

The results

Across 40 evaluated models, WildBench's metrics correlate with Chatbot Arena's human-voted Elo (Hard-English split, May 20 2024 snapshot) better than every prior automatic benchmark the authors compare against: WB-Reward reaches a Pearson correlation of 0.98 and WB-Score reaches 0.95 on top-ranking models, versus Arena-Hard's 0.91 and AlpacaEval 2.0's 0.87 (0.89 for the length-controlled version).

A sample of the live leaderboard (WB-Reward-Mix, no length penalty, vs Chatbot Arena Elo where available):

ModelWB-Reward (Mix)WB-ScoreArena Elo
GPT-4o-051335.765.31293
GPT-4-Turbo-040934.664.71251
Llama-3-70B-Instruct21.060.41213
Claude 3 Opus20.163.11232
Qwen1.5-72B-Chat4.456.51143
GPT-3.5-turbo-0125−33.542.11105
Llama-2-7B-chat−48.027.61012

Length robustness check: Llama-2-70B-chat and Llama-3-70B-Instruct produce almost identical output lengths (2,965 vs 2,983 characters), yet rank 33rd vs 5th of 40 models. Yi-1.5-6B-Chat has the 4th-longest outputs in the field (3,322 characters) but ranks 29th. The authors read this as evidence that "response quality [is] the most important factor in the evaluation process," not length.

Ablations: removing the checklist from the prompt drops WB-Score's Pearson correlation from 0.925 to 0.905 (all-models). Sweeping the length-penalty threshold K over {100, 200, 500, 1000, ∞} finds K=500 best. Swapping the judge across GPT-4, Claude-3-Opus, and Mistral-Large "produced very similar results" with "minimal influence" on relative rankings — the paper recommends a single judge in production for cost and speed, despite validating with three.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

WildBench's real contribution — sourcing genuinely diverse, hard, multi-turn tasks from a million real conversations rather than author-written or narrowly-scraped prompts — is solid and shows up clearly in Figure 3's category breakdown. But several of the numbers deserve a second look before taking the 0.98 headline at face value.

The headline 0.98/0.91/0.89/0.87 comparison is the friendliest cut of Table 3. Those numbers are the top-6-model P-Cortop column, which the authors justify because "models released in the future are likely to compete with the top models" — a defensible rationale, but also the column where WildBench's margin over Arena-Hard is largest (0.984 vs 0.909). On the larger, less cherry-pickable 14-model P-Corall comparison, the gap narrows: WB-Reward(Mix) sits at 0.973–0.976 versus Arena-Hard's 0.925 and AlpacaEval2's 0.952 — still ahead, but by a smaller margin than the abstract implies, and AlpacaEval2's raw (non-length-controlled) P-Corall of 0.952 is closer to WildBench's than the abstract's headline framing suggests.

The length penalty only guards the borderline case. The K-character margin converts "slightly better/worse" verdicts to ties when the length gap exceeds K — but "much better/worse" verdicts pass through completely untouched, regardless of length gap. A judge that's been swayed into a confident "much better" verdict by response length alone is not caught by this mechanism at all; it's a tiebreaker for close calls, not a general length-debiasing regression like the contemporaneous LC-AlpacaEval's.

Curation and grading lean on the same small set of judge models. GPT-4-Turbo, Claude-3-Sonnet, and Claude-3-Opus jointly decide task difficulty during curation; GPT-4-Turbo is both the default WB-Score judge and one of WB-Reward's three baselines; GPT-4-Turbo and Claude-3-Opus jointly draft the checklists. The paper's own multi-judge ablation (GPT-4 / Claude-3-Opus / Mistral-Large) found rankings stable across judges — a genuine robustness check — but the deployed leaderboard "recommend[s] using a single LLM as a judge in practice," meaning the production benchmark doesn't carry forward the exact multi-judge setup that was validated.

"Dynamic" is aspirational for the artifact you'd actually download. WildBench markets itself as periodically refreshed (V1 → V2 already happened) with tasks withheld from WildChat's public release — real anti-leakage measures. But Table 1 itself marks WildBench's chat history as "Static," and the released V2 is, once downloaded, exactly as fixed and memorizable as MT-Bench's 80 questions until a V3 ships on some future, unscheduled cadence.

baselines

Compared against MT-Bench, AlpacaEval2(-LC), and Arena-Hard on the same Chatbot Arena Hard-En-240520 Elo reference — a fair, apples-to-apples setup. But the reported 0.98/0.95 headline is the top-6-model P-Cortop column; the fuller 14-model P-Corall shows a narrower lead (0.973–0.976 vs Arena-Hard's 0.925, AlpacaEval2's 0.952).

contamination

Real mitigations exist (tasks withheld from the public WildChat release, V1→V2 refresh), but the released V2 benchmark is itself a static, public 1,024-task set once downloaded — "dynamic" describes the project's cadence, not a property of any single release.

what's missed

The length penalty only converts "slightly better/worse" outcomes to ties, leaving "much better/worse" verdicts unguarded against length. The validated 3-judge ensemble isn't what the recommended single-judge production leaderboard actually runs.

would raise confidence

Report the full P-Corall table alongside the top-6 headline; extend the length penalty to discount "much better/worse" outcomes too, not just near-ties; publish a committed re-curation cadence rather than an ad hoc V1→V2 refresh; ship the validated multi-judge ensemble as the deployed default.

// bottom line

WildBench earns its place as the most task-diverse, real-usage-grounded automatic chat benchmark of its generation, and its correlation advantage over MT-Bench/AlpacaEval/Arena-Hard is real. But read the 0.98 Pearson figure as the top-model-subset number it is, not a universal correlation, and note that both its length-bias fix and its judge setup are narrower in the deployed leaderboard than in the paper's most careful ablations.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Real usage logs, not author-written prompts. WildBench mines 1,024 hard, multi-turn tasks (up to 5 turns, 978.5-char average query) from over a million real WildChat conversations, filtered for length/language/toxicity/near-duplicates and difficulty-screened by three LLM judges, landing a far more balanced 12-category mix than AlpacaEval (50% info-seeking) or Arena-Hard (57% coding).
  2. Two checklist-guided metrics. WB-Score (1–10 individual grading, rescaled as (S−5)×2) and WB-Reward (5-outcome pairwise against 3 tiered baselines, with a K=500-character length-penalty for near-ties) both use per-instance checklists co-drafted by GPT-4-Turbo and Claude-3-Opus to reduce single-judge subjectivity.
  3. Strong correlation with human Elo. WB-Reward hits 0.98 Pearson and WB-Score 0.95 against Chatbot Arena's Hard-English Elo on top models, ahead of Arena-Hard's 0.91 and AlpacaEval 2.0's 0.87 (0.89 length-controlled) — with a concrete length-robustness check (near-identical-length Llama-2-70B and Llama-3-70B rank 33rd vs 5th of 40).
  4. The 0.98 headline is the friendliest subset, and the length fix only guards near-ties. The full 14-model correlation table shows a narrower margin over Arena-Hard than the top-6 headline; "much better/worse" verdicts pass through the length penalty untouched; and the production leaderboard runs a single judge rather than the multi-judge ensemble the paper validated.
